data.title: LVMH: Hope Is Here; More Is Needed
data.description:
Luxury fashion giant LVMH Moët Hennessy (OTCPK:LVMUY) (OTCPK:LVMHF) might have had a poor start to 2025, but the second half of the year is proving to be positive for the stock. Since I last checked in late July, it's up by 23%. It was meaningfully supported by the release of its third quarter trading update earlier this week, which saw it gain ~9% on the day.

This calls for a closer look at the update, with a view to develop a more holistic perspective on LVMH at a time when the broader conditions remain challenged.

Green shoots of turnaround...

There's certainly some good news in the update. After a YoY revenue contraction in the first two quarters of the year, LVMH finally reported a small uptick in Q3 2025. Organic revenue growth at constant currency rose by 1% YoY during the quarter, which is a welcome change from the revenue erosion seen in recent quarters. Further, a look at the details of the update underlines two specific encouraging signs, which are as follows.

  • Selective retailing sees solid pickup

Up to the first half (H1 2025), only one of the company's five revenue segments reported some growth. Of the remaining four, two were flat and the other two saw revenue contraction. In Q3 2025, however, all of them, save Fashion & Leather Goods, saw a YoY revenue expansion.

Selective Retailing is the biggest gainer of the quarter, with a 7% YoY increase. This is an important development since the segment is the biggest revenue stream for LVMH after Fashion & Leather Goods. It brought in over 21% of the revenues in the first nine months of 2025 (9M 2025).

  • Asia ex-Japan revenues pickup

From a geographical standpoint, LVMH's recovery in the Asia ex-Japan market is a sure relief, as it indicates improving trends in the biggest global luxury market, China. The geography is also the company's largest market, accounting for 27% of the revenues in 9M 2025. With its 2% YoY increase in Q3 2025, it snapped out of a notable drag in the two quarters before (see slide below).

Similarly, the turnaround in the US market is also quite encouraging given its significance for LVMH. It's right behind Asia ex-Japan, as it's the second biggest market for the company, with a 25% contribution to revenues in 9M 2025.

...but concerns persist...

However, the bigger picture is still concerning. This is not just because of LVMH's weak performance over 9M 2025 but also because of unsupportive macroeconomics and the luxury industry. Here are the two key ones:

  • Persisting softness in key pockets for LVMH

With the company's revenues having shrunk by 2% YoY for 9M 2025, it's hard to see any expansion in them for the full year 2025. Moreover, the company's biggest revenue segment, Fashion & Leather Goods, is still in contraction mode. Sure, its extent of decline reduced to 2% YoY in Q3 FY 2025 from 7% YoY in H1 2025. But considering that the segment brings in half of LVMH's revenues, this is still a somewhat worrisome sign.

Further, two of the company's key markets, Europe and Japan, are still weak. They might not be as big as the US and Asia ex-Japan markets in terms of revenue contribution, but they still bring in a substantial 26% of the total figure. Of these, the bigger European market is a particular concern, considering that both this year and the next, its GDP growth is expected to be at sub-trend levels of just over 1%.

  • Uncertain economic and industry conditions

It's hardly just Europe that's seeing weakness either. The IMF expects marginal softening in world output as such over the next two years in a "landscape marked by greater protectionism and fragmentation", the fund notes.

While the luxury market tends to be resilient even in the face of macroeconomic softness, it's unlikely to be the case this time around. In my recent article on the British trench coat brand Burberry (OTCPK:BURBY), I had pointed out a host of factors affecting the market right now (see segment on "Luxury markets remain unsupportive..." in the link).

Coupled with uncertain macros, including China's weakness, reasons including fast price rises during the recent boom years, which aren't seen as commensurate with the offerings, luxury shopping fatigue, and even luxury shame are factors affecting the market.

...and market multiples aren't fully supportive

LVMUY's market multiples aren't supportive either. I had earlier projected that the company's net income will land at $13.1 billion in 2025. If the estimate were to remain unchanged, the forward non-GAAP P/E is now at 23.72x, up from 21.71x back in July, due to the price rise in the interim. The present forward P/E is then slightly higher than the stock's five-year average of 22.05x.

However, after the latest update, the estimates do require a relook. At that time, I had assumed that LVMH's revenues would drop by 3% in 2025, in line with the number seen up to H1 2025. This drag is reduced to 2% instead, as seen in 9M 2025. The net margin kept unchanged at 14%, lower than the 14.8% seen in 2024, factoring in the potential impact of tariffs. This results in a profit figure rising to $13.6 billion and a forward P/E of 22.86x. However, even this P/E is higher than the stock's five-year average.

With 2025 almost at an end, though, it's also a good idea to make an income estimate for 2026. If revenue growth were to improve to 1%, the same as that for Q3 2025, and the net margin goes back up to 14.8%, as seen in 2024, the net income comes to $14.5 billion. This results in a forward P/E of 21.38x, which is an improvement but doesn't make a Buy case for LVMUY.

What next?

No matter how I look at it, the stock story clearly indicates that LVMH is one to Hold right now. Even as some recovery is underway, it's just too limited so far. And with the broader macro and industry conditions still uncertain, it's hard to vouch that the recovery will continue uninterrupted. Still, if the market multiples were low enough, there could be a Buy case. But even they have run ahead of themselves.

There could yet be surprises, though. For example, if LVMH sees a margin expansion or if the festive season this year turns out to be bigger than in 2024. But the trends so far don't suggest them, though a gradual recovery is possible. I'm retaining a Hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Bancorp: Downgrade To Hold Despite A Solid Q3 Due To Share Price Recovery
data.description:
Introduction

The super-regional bank U.S. Bancorp (NYSE:USB) announced its results for the third quarter of 2025 today, against the backdrop of a continuing difficult macroeconomic environment and after the Federal Reserve recently lowered its benchmark interest rate by 0.25 percentage points – the first cut since December 2024.

U.S. Bancorp is one of the banks I review more or less regularly here on Seeking Alpha (overview here), the last time back in mid-April, when I upgraded USB stock to Buy, when it was still weighed down by tariff-related fears. Since then, the broader market and U.S. Bancorp have recovered, and at the time of writing, USB shares are trading at around $47, which is about 25% higher than when I published my last article.

In this brief update, I share my take on the bank's latest results and my longer-term view of the investment as part of my broadly diversified portfolio.

U.S. Bancorp Q3 2025 Earnings: Continued Solid Performance, But I Am Still Downgrading To Hold

U.S. Bancorp beat both on GAAP earnings per share and revenue consensus. Personally, I don't pay too much attention to such short-term earnings and revenue surprises, but I was pleased to see that USB's latest EPS of $1.22 (up 18% year-over-year) were not influenced by one-offs. Revenues rose by nearly 7% compared to the third quarter of 2024, to approximately $7.3 billion.

What led to these - in my view solid - results?

I would not go so far as to attribute the performance in the third quarter solely to the new CEO Gunjan Kedia, who succeeded Andy Cecere in mid-April. Although loan growth improved in the third quarter compared to both the previous quarter (+0.2%) and Q3 2024 (+1.4%) (Figure 1), these are obviously not spectacular figures and clearly indicate the ongoing reluctance for investments by companies and the private sector.

Deposits rose by 1.8% compared to the second quarter (Figure 2) and by 0.6% year-over-year. This was reassuring to see, especially given the decline in short-term interest rates and the negative trend since the fourth quarter of 2024. According to management, lower rates paid on interest-bearing deposits during the quarter contributed to net income growth, besides a favorable loan mix and fixed asset repricing.

From a longer-term perspective, however, it makes more sense to describe deposits as very stable since the acquisition of MUFG Union Bank. Given the normalization of the yield curve and the associated attractiveness of longer-term fixed-income investments, this is certainly a positive development.

Figure 3 shows a historical review of the yield curve over the last 30 months, based on daily Treasury par yield curve rates as a proxy. In addition to the increasing attractiveness of long-term fixed-income investments, the normalization tells us that U.S. banks are increasingly able to generate a positive earnings contribution via maturity transformation (explanation in this article). Combined with the general trend toward leaner and more efficient processes, this should be seen a positive sign for the longer-term earnings prospects of the entire sector (ignoring credit risk provisions, of course).

The improved operating efficiency is in part a consequence of the historical anomalies of a zero interest rate environment, and while U.S. Bancorp could have done better in recent years, we should not forget the industry-wide challenges related to personnel costs since 2022. Nevertheless, U.S. Bancorp's efficiency ratio improved by a strong 2.0 percentage points compared to the second quarter, after and already solid improvement by 1.6 percentage points in that quarter (Figure 4). In my view, this is where the new CEO's initiatives are most visible, and it is encouraging to see the bank approaching its previously stated medium-term target of 55% with good momentum.

Leaving goodwill and other intangible assets aside, USB's efficiency ratio was 55.5% for the quarter, an improvement of 2.0 percentage points compared to the previous quarter and 2.7 percentage points compared to Q3 2024. In the context of tangible equity, USB's continued solid return (ROTCE) of 18.6% in Q3 2025 is noteworthy. This is already slightly above the former CEO's medium-term target, but is not surprising given reported stable operating expenses despite strong top-line growth. The positive (adjusted) operating leverage of 530 basis points was definitely encouraging, especially since it was largely due to higher fee income and not, for example, a decline in tangible book value. In fact, U.S. Bancorp's tangible equity increased by 4.6% compared to the previous quarter and by 12.8% compared to Q3 2024.

Finally, USB's credit quality remains solid, as evidenced by the low and largely stable non-performing assets ratio (0.43% of total loans and other real estate, Figure 5) and the charge-off ratio of 0.58%, which is largely in line with the domestic average (Figure 6, third quarter number not yet published).

Against the backdrop of what I consider to be conservative lending practices and solid operating results, but also in view of the further strengthened capitalization (USB's CET1 ratio now stands at 10.9%, an increase of 30 basis points compared to the previous quarter), I consider the 4% dividend increase announced in early September to be entirely justified (not to say expected). Management also continued its share buyback program, repurchasing around 8 million shares so far this year and 2 million in Q3. Excluding shares issued for stock inventive plans and other purposes, the number of outstanding shares at the end of the third quarter of 2025 was 1,556 million, 4 million fewer than at the end of 2024.

In conclusion, I believe that U.S. Bancorp's third quarter 2025 results were reassuring from both a short-term and longer-term perspective. While the results were not spectacular, the strong growth in fee-related income, stable operating expenses, and continued stable credit quality metrics in particular confirm my investment thesis of owning U.S. Bancorp stock for the long term.

However, given the recovery in the share price by 25% since my last article and the lack of compelling arguments that would justify a more bullish stance, I will not be increasing my position in USB stock at this time. Figure 7, which illustrates U.S. Bancorp's price to book value and price to tangible book value over the past five years, shows that the shares are still trading at a small discount.

However, given what I believe to be a not fully justified broad market recovery, ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties, and an expected modest weaking in credit quality metrics due to likely higher-for-longer long-term interest rates (over which the Fed has little influence), I am downgrading USB stock to Hold. However, if volatility increases again and the premiums for USB put options with an exercise price in the low $40 range become reasonable again (i.e., an implied annualized return of 15%+), I could imagine selling a few contracts.

Thank you very much for reading my latest article. Whether you agree or disagree with my conclusions, I always welcome your opinion and feedback in the comments below. And if there's anything I should improve or expand on in future articles, drop me a line as well. As always, please consider this article only as a first step in your own due diligence.

data.title: Alphabet: The AI Leader OpenAI Is Still Chasing (But Will Never Catch)
data.description:
When Elon Musk, Sam Altman, and others co-founded OpenAI back in 2015 they said the reason was to create a non-profit to develop artificial intelligence "in the way that is most likely to benefit humanity as a whole." However, the real reason may well have had much more to do with business and to beat Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (from here on out I will refer to "Google" instead of Alphabet). After all, at the time Google was considered to be the global leader in AI technology. I won't get into the tumultuous relationship and seemingly endless feud that evolved between Musk and Altman since Musk left OpenAI in 2018 - you can click on the "beat Alphabet" link in the previous sentence for a summary of that. What I will discuss today, 10-years after OpenAI's founding, is why - in totality - Google is still the leading AI company that OpenAI continues to chase - and why OpenAI will likely never catch Google, and why Google will continue to deliver excellent financial and shareholder returns for many years to come.

Investment Thesis: The AI Leader

Whenever I leave a comment on Seeking Alpha saying that, in totality, Google is arguably the global leader in AI, I typically get a response like this one: "What are you talking about? OpenAI came out with ChatGPT and caught Google totally flat-footed." I won't argue the fact that OpenAI beat Google to market with a chatbot. There's no doubt about that, right? However, what I will argue with is that that accomplishment alone somehow makes OpenAI "the leader in AI". That is a simply a faulty conclusion in my opinion. Let's take a look at the facts.

Way back in December of 2020, I wrote a Seeking Alpha article on Google (see: Next Stop $2100) in which I highlighted a ground-breaking accomplishment made by Google AI's Deep Mind unit:

On November 30, Deep Mind - Google's AI Lab - announced it had cracked the code on the "protein-folding problem" with a neural-network based algorithm called Alpha Fold. This is considered to be a major breakthrough that evolutionary biologist Andrei Lupus told Nature is "a game changer. This will change medicine. It will change research. It will change bioengineering. It will change everything."

Here we are - five years later - and I ask all of you: what contribution has ChaptGPT or OpenAI made that comes even remotely close to the significance and impact that this single contribution by Google's Deep Mind has had on humanity? You can't. Because the simple fact is that there is none.

AI Specific Processors

You have no doubt read about the plethora of multi-billion dollar deals that OpenAI has recently made with a number of leading cloud, neo-cloud, and processor companies including:

  • Oracle (ORCL)
  • NVIDIA (NVDA)
  • AMD (AMD)
  • CoreWeave (CRWV)
  • Broadcom (AVGO)

Considering that OpenAI is still not profitable, this has led to much speculation about circular financing, comparisons to the dot.com bubble, etc., etc. What isn't debatable is that OpenAI is on a mission to grow its overall compute power. That's because it is absolutely critical for OpenAI to train its foundational LLMs (i.e. ChatGPT) and for cost-effective inference to monetize its models. But what is most interesting to me is that OpenAI is taking a page right out of Google's playbook: it has partnered with Broadcom in order to gain in-house ASIC design expertise (i.e. to design a customized & optimized Broadcom "XPU"). Further, OpenAI plans to bundle its XPU to Broadcom's leading-edge high-speed networking equipment in order to "scale-up and scale-out" its AI data centers. In the recent Broadcom press release (on Monday, October 13th) announcement, OpenAI co-founder and CEO Sam Altman said:

Partnering with Broadcom is a critical step in building the infrastructure needed to unlock AI’s potential and deliver real benefits for people and businesses. Developing our own accelerators adds to the broader ecosystem of partners all building the capacity required to push the frontier of AI to provide benefits to all humanity.

A more accurate statement by Altman might have simply admitted "OpenAI feels we have to develop customized ASICs with Broadcom and scale-up and scale-out our networking infrastructure in order to more effectively compete with Google." After all, as I pointed out in my Seeking Alpha piece, Broadcom: The 'Secret Sauce' Behind Its Booming XPU Business, Google was the first company to partner with Broadcom on an optimized AI processor way back in 2014 (i.e., a decade ago and a year before OpenAI was founded). Indeed, Google is now on its 7th (or arguably 8th) generation of its Tensor Processor Units (i.e. what Google calls "TPUs"). Google also has a Pixel Neural Core and even a specific processor to run machine learning ("ML") models for edge-computing, called the "Edge TPU", which was released way back in 2018 (the year Musk left OpenAI).

Indeed, Google has been very successful in deploying its in-house TPU design and marrying them with NVIDIA GPUs within its data centers. This is how Google CEO Sundar Pichai summed it up during the company's Q2 conference call:

First, AI infrastructure. We operate the leading global network of AI-optimized data centers and cloud regions. We also offer the industry's widest range of TPUs and GPUs along with storage and software built on top. That's why nearly all GenAI unicorns use Google Cloud. And that's why a growing number, including leading AI research labs like Safe Superintelligence and Physical Intelligence use TPU specifically.

My point here is that Google has far-n-away more experience (indeed, over a decade more...) in designing AI specific processors as compared to OpenAI - which still has yet to have its very first in-house XPU in-hand. That said, I think OpenAI made a very smart decision to partner with Broadcom to fuel its attempt to catch Google (and, full-disclosure, as a Broadcom shareholder, I certainly appreciate that!). Yet for those paying attention, this is a page right out of Google's very successful playbook. The only difference is that Google wrote the playbook and began executing it about a decade ago, and the company has been benefiting from all that work. As Pichai also said on the Q2 conference call:

We are bringing AI to all our users and partners through surfaces like Workspace, Chrome and more. The growth in usage has been incredible. At IO in May, we announced that we processed 480 trillion monthly tokens across our surfaces. Since then, we have doubled that number, now processing over 980 trillion monthly tokens, a remarkable increase.

Servers

Google built its first production server rack at Stanford way back in 1998 (see a cool photo of it in this Wiki article). Google began building processor clusters back in 2003. In 2006, Google built what might be considered one of the first modern day style data centers in The Dalles, Oregon (i.e. close to hydroelectric power). It was the size of two football fields and had cooling towers four stories high.

As the previously referenced Wiki article pointed out, there is no official record of how much data center capacity Google currently has. However, that article referenced a Gartner report from way back in 2016 that estimated Google had 2.5 million servers. My point here is that, just as with AI specific processors, Google has many years of experience building-out its hyperscaler data-center capacity while OpenAI has arguably only just begun. As pointed out in my last Seeking Alpha piece (Amazon: Don't Discount The Seemingly Forgotten Hyperscaler), Google Cloud was the fastest growing of the three leading hyperscalers in Q2 and GC now has an annual run-rate of over $50 billion. So, OpenAI clearly has its work cut out for it.

And of course Google is by no means standing still. Indeed, last quarter Google announced it was increasing its FY25 cap-ex by $10 billion to $85 billion due to "strong and growing demand for our Cloud products and services." So, while OpenAI continues to grab all the headlines, Google continues to keep its head-down and quietly do what it has always done: adding leading-edge AI data center capacity. This includes - just this month - a recently announced $15 billion "data center and AI-hub" in India and $9 billion of AI capacity expansion in South Carolina.

Size & Scale

The Broadcom/OpenAI release referenced earlier said:

OpenAI has grown to over 800 million weekly active users and strong adoption across global enterprises, small businesses, and developers. This collaboration will help OpenAI advance its mission to ensure that artificial general intelligence benefits all of humanity [Author's Note: add "and compete against Google".]

So, I am clear-eyed about OpenAI's accomplishments, its potential, and its lofty valuation - which was recently estimated at ~$500 billion. No doubt ChatGPT was arguably the fastest deployment of a new generation of technology in the history of man. You will get no argument from me on that.

However, now let's take a look at Google, which as of Tuesday's close has a market-cap just under $3 trillion (i.e. ~6x that of OpenAI). Google also has a chatbot similar to ChatGPT (Gemini AI Assistant). In fact, Google recently deployed Gemini on 3.5-4 billion (yes, with a "b") Chrome, Pixel, and Android devices across the planet. Earlier this month, Google deployed Gemini AI across 800 million smart home devices. Rather than re-write all the various platform-level deployments of Gemini AI, I'll copy & paste a list from SQ Magazine's article Google Gemini AI Statistics 2025: User Growth, Enterprise Use & Model Benchmarks:

Pretty impressive, right? Especially the 2nd line (Gemini over taking Bing Chat in terms of daily usage volume). I say that because both Bing Chat and ChatGPT were built with OpenAI LLMs. Now, to be fair, OpenAI's ChatGPT is still king-of-the-hill in when it comes to global generative AI chatbot market share:

Well, hmmm... look at Perplexity, less than half the market share of Google's Gemini? I still remember a year or so ago when it was predicted that Perplexity would overtake Google in a couple months? Well, that narrative obviously didn't last very long, did it?

The fact is that OpenAI has to dramatically and quickly grow its compute power in order to come close to competing with Google. That's because it's still relatively a one-trick pony: ChatGPT. So, it requires the compute power to train the never-ending need for the next leading-edge LLM (i.e. ChatGPT) and to use inference (i.e. the in-house Broadcom XPU) so that ChatGPT can answer queries efficiently, quickly, accurately, and with low power. OpenAI needs to do so because Google has been doing exactly that for many years and is considered to do so the quickest and using the least amount of power in the industry (primarily because of its large use of custom TPUs that are optimized specifically for Google's algorithms and are therefore more compute- and power-efficient). The TPU clusters are then tied together with Broadcom's high-speed networking products in an overall architecture that is optimized for Google's AI workloads. Companies that try to use a $30,000 Nvidia GPU for inferencing are going to be significantly disadvantaged as compared to Google performing inferencing using its in-house TPUs (designed in partnership with Broadcom).

Robotaxis

Meantime, note that Google's Waymo is clobbering Tesla in the robotaxi market. Forbes ran what I found to be an unbiased report that summed up well The Deep Story On The Waymo Vs Tesla Robotaxi Battle in which it mentioned Google's advantage with AI TPUs and also said:

Overwhelmingly, Tesla’s biggest problem is that their current self-driving platform can’t self-drive.

I've been saying that for some time in my Seeking Alpha commentary. I mean, at one point the comparison was:

  • Waymo: 10 million+ paid robotaxi rides across multiple large U.S. cities: San Francisco, Silicon Valley, Los Angeles, Phoenix, and Austin.
  • Tesla: 0 paid robotaxi rides across Austin

(Author: I don't count it as a "robotaxi ride" if there is a Tesla employee in the car. To me, that is a glorified Yellow Cab with a Tesla sticker on it).

Just this week, Seeking Alpha reported that Waymo plans to launch its robotaxis in London next year. This will be Waymo's first European and second international market after Tokyo. Giving a slight jab at Tesla, the company said.

We’re bringing our fully autonomous ride-hailing service across the pond, where we intend to offer rides – with no human behind the wheel – in 2026.

As I have also been pointing out in my Seeking Alpha comments, the problem here is very likely the technology platform Musk & Tesla chose many years ago: camera only. As compared to Waymo's "camera+LiDAR" technology, it appears that one is actually ready for prime-time autonomous-driving (i.e. Waymo) and that Tesla's supposed "FSD" is arguably not.

Tobotaxis are yet another area in which a louder competitor (in this case, Elon Musk and Tesla) sucks all the air out of the room but, once again, there is Google quietly going about doing the hard engineering work to actually make it happen. Meantime, it's very interesting to me that Seeking Alpha reported recently that an analyst at Citi is now saying that the $10 billion order placed with Broadcom last quarter - that many investors & analysts (including yours truly) wrongly assumed was OpenAI - is now thought to have been Elon Musk's 'xAI'. That makes perfect sense: Elon has likely and finally figured out that Tesla's robotaxis don't have a chance against Waymo's without Musk having an in-house AI TPU. Again, that would be much to the benefit of Broadcom. However, I suspect that until Musk admits that Tesla's camera-only autonomous platform needs to add LiDAR technology in order for Tesla to produce an actually safe robotaxi, it won't matter what AI processor Tesla and/or xAI uses because it won't have a genuine robotaxi and Google's Waymo will continue to eat its lunch. OpenAI won't catch Google... and neither will Musk's 'xAI'.

My point with all these seemingly random stats and observations is that size & scale matter. So while OpenAI is focused on building out AI data-centers for the benefit of evolving its foundational ChatGPT LLM, Google is building out AI data centers to be used across its entire platform:

  • Google Search
  • Google Network
  • YouTube (the #1 streaming service on the planet)
  • Google Cloud
  • Waymo Robotaxis
  • Android OS (Chrome browsers, Pixel smartphones, home devices, etc)

In my opinion, the size and scale of Google's wide-moat platform is an inherent strength and massive advantage that Google has over OpenAI.

Earnings

At the end of the day, all that really matters (at least to me) is consistently and relentlessly growing revenue, earnings, and free-cash-flow per share. OpenAI has chosen to stay private. I can't blame them - any quarterly earnings report comparison with Google's would only highlight Google's high profitability and OpenAI's losses. Besides, it's smart... I mean, if you can gain a $500 billion valuation level without having ever earned a penny of profit, why rock-the-boat, right?

But Google's financial performance is out there for all to see, and its Q3 report is due out on October 29th. But let's take a quick look back at Google's Q2's results:

As you can see, and despite the narrative started a couple years ago that Perplexity and ChatGPT would somehow "kill" Google Search, just the opposite took place: Google Search not only grew, but supported by AI Mode and AI Overviews it grew at a strong double-digit rate (11.7%).

In addition, Google Cloud posted the fastest growth in Q2 (+31.7%) as compared to the other top hyperscalers: Amazon and Microsoft (MSFT). Better still, GC operating income was $2.83 billion - which more than doubled yoy while operating margin of 20.7% was up a whopping 9.4 full percentage points on a yoy basis (see slide 7 of Google's Q2 presentation).

Google's Q2 net income was $28.2 billion (+19% yoy) while EPS came in at $2.31 (+22% yoy).

Over the past 12-months - and despite an accelerated FY25 cap-ex spending plan of $85 billion (+60% yoy) - Google generated a whopping $66.7 billion in free-cash-flow, or an estimated $5.47/share based on the average of 12.2 billion outstanding fully diluted share-count at the end of Q2. Please see my last Seeking Alpha article on Google in July of 2023: Q2 Surprise: Google Generated $2 Billion More Free-Cash-Flow Than Microsoft.

Better still, Google ended the quarter with $95.1 billion in cash & cash equivalents (or an estimated $7.80/share).

For the upcoming Q3 report, current analysts consensus earnings (courtesy of Yahoo.Finance) are as follows:

As you can see, the current EPS estimate of $2.30/share would be +8.5% yoy. However, more importantly investors should note how much Google spent on cap-ex during the quarter and the resulting reduction in free-cash-flow. As you can see from a slide in the Q2 presentation, FCF was a still solid $5.3 billion, but that was -61% yoy:

Clearly, Google's FCF profile will suffer as it ramps up spending on AI infrastructure, but that sure beats giving away equity or taking on debt, right?

My point here is that Google is not only a free-cash-flow generating machine, it is cash rich and can easily afford the massive AI data center build-out it is currently embarked on in order to insure it remains the global leader in AI. On the other hand, note that OpenAI is getting financial assistance for its massive "hurry-up and catch-up" AI infrastructure build-out. As Seeking Alpha reported:

Softbank, which owns nearly 90% of Arm, has also invested heavily in the ChatGPT maker. The Japanese conglomerate has reportedly been securing a margin loan backed by its Arm shares to finance further investments in OpenAI. Softbank is helping OpenAI develop and finance its ambitious data center plan.

So this is another indication that OpenAI won't catch Google: debt and margin loans are arguably helping OpenAI with its build-out whereas Google is easily capable of funding its AI capacity expansion spending through the enormous cash-flow thrown off by the operations of its strong global platform.

Meantime, an article in the WSJ's Heard on the Street column reports that OpenAI's revenue this year is expected to be ~$13 billion. That means OpenAI's total annual revenue in 2025 will be less than 15% of Google's $96.4 billion in Q2 quarterly revenue. That article also reported that OpenAI has told investors it won't be profitable until 2029. Just imagine how much free-cash-flow Google is going to generate, how much more AI infrastructure Google will build, and how much bigger Google Cloud will be before OpenAI even earns a nickel! Again, OpenAI clearly has a long (long!) way to go to catch Google... and I say it won't ever do so.

Risks and Catalysts

The primary risk to Google stock continues to be valuation discounts due to the perceived - or potentially real - threat AI disruption is to its Search business. For instance, just this week Seeking Alpha reported that Wal Mart (WMT) has partnered with OpenAI to use ChatGPT for customers to shop online at Walmart using Instant Checkout. On Tuesday, Wal Mart was up nearly 5% on the news. That said, there is nothing preventing Google from using Gemini AI to reach similar retail partnerships ... considering the likely very small margins such agreements likely generate, it is questionable whether or not they would be worth Google's time.

Considering the many years-long and highly successful partnership between Google & Apple (AAPL) (i.e. Google paying Apple to be the default Search engine on Apple's iPhones), the fact that Google has a ton of experience with deploying Gemini AI on its Pixel smartphones, and that Apple appears to be significantly lagging in AI, I expect Google & Apple to make a deal enabling iPhones to be shipped with Google's Gemini AI (and Search). In my opinion, that would be a huge upside catalyst for both companies. That's because while the benefit to Google is clear, so too would the size of the check Google would hand over to Apple.

Summary & Conclusions

In totality, Google is arguably still the leading AI company on the planet and is the company that OpenAI began competing with from its initial founding, and the company that OpenAI is still chasing. But OpenAI won't catch Google. And it won't catch Google because Google's overall technology lead is substantial and because OpenAI's platform simply doesn't have near the size, scale, and diversity as compared to Google's.

Google currently trades with a TTM P/E of only 26.3x - which despite all the stats above from the Q2 earnings report, is actually a 3.8% discount to the S&P500 (P/E=30.8x). For a company that just grew Q2 EPS by 22% yoy, has $95+ billion in cash, and generated $66+ billion in free-cash-flow over the past 12-months, the valuation discount of GOOG relative to the S&P500 (let alone its big-tech peers), is completely irrational and somewhat comical in my opinion. That being the case, and as much as I love Broadcom, Google is my #1 big-tech pick here and I rate it a STRONG BUY. As the market begins to understand Google is still the global AI leader and the success it has had monetizing AI across its very impressive platform (including Google Cloud), I expect GOOG's valuation discount to erode heading into FY26. Not only should GOOG stock begin to trade at an S&P500 multiple, but in my opinion it has earned a premium to the S&P500 and should trade at a P/E in the range of 30-35x.

Yet with no change at all in GOOG's current multiple, my 12-month price target on GOOG stock is $295/share (+20% from Tuesday's close), based on GOOG's cash-rich balance sheet, free-cash-flow profile, its rapidly expanding Google Cloud margin, Gemini AI's success, Google's deeply entrenched advertising business, and the company's ability to monetize AI across its entire stellar and wide-moat platform.

I'll end with a chart of the five-year total returns of the top-3 hyperscalers and will add Broadcom and the S&P500 as additional references:

As you can see from the graphic, Broadcom has been far and away the leader of the companies chosen for comparisons (for the reasons outlined in this article). However, note that Google has bested the returns of all others in the comparison by a significant margin. I expect both Google and Amazon to vastly improve their performances over the coming 5-years and I hope that OpenAI will go public in that time frame so that investors can fairly and accurately compare OpenAI's financials to Google's. I predict it won't even be close.

data.title: Sumitomo Mitsui: All Eyes On Capital Recycling
data.description:
I'm still positive about Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group, Inc. (NYSE:SMFG) (8316.T). I like how it is extracting cash from certain assets and re-distributing the proceeds to high-growth business areas.

My prior August 4, 2025, update touched on SMFG's above-expectations 1QFY25 (YE end-March '26) bottom line.

Minority Investment Exit And Affiliated Company IPO Draw Attention

SMFG has recently made two key asset monetization moves.

Last month, the enterprise issued an announcement disclosing the divestiture of its "entire 1.65% stake in (India's) Kotak Mahindra Bank."

The group has realized a ¥65 billion gain (net of tax) from the above-mentioned deal, according to management's remarks at a Sep 22 investor call (S&P Capital IQ transcript). The consideration represents a meaningful 5% of Sumitomo's existing full-year earnings guide (JPY 1.3 trillion).

I noted that SMFG's "cautious full-year guidance could be revised upward" thanks to "improving U.S.-Japan trade clarity and likely BOJ rate hikes" in my Aug-04 update. The probability of this bullish scenario materializing has become even higher due to Kotak's equity interest sale.

Also, its "Common Equity Tier 1" or "CET1" metric has been boosted by 7bps (basis points) after parting ways with this Indian bank. This frees up surplus capital that could be utilized elsewhere, which I will outline later.

Separately, SMFG's 29%-owned associate called "Infcurion" has obtained regulatory approval to be publicly listed on Japan's "TSE Growth Market" in late Oct this year.

Infcurion's Business Operations At A Glance

There are little details available regarding the IPO. But it's reasonable to think that Infucrion should be self-financing post-listing, given the ability to raise funds from the capital markets independently. This implies that further cash injections from its parent wouldn't be required.

SMFG could also have sold part of the fintech arm's shares, assuming it's a secondary offering. Even if that isn't the case, the bank will have many chances to monetize its stake on the stock exchange subsequently.

In the next section, I highlight Sumitomo's capital reinvestment initiatives, which were made possible by Kotak's and Infucrion's latest developments detailed here.

Focus On Stake Increases In American And Indian Financial Institutions

SMFG is investing fresh money to exploit synergies with its investees "Yes Bank Limited (YBL)" and "Jefferies (JEF)."

My earlier May 19, 2025, piece indicated that Sumitomo's "coverage of the Indian banking market will be widened" in the "mid-sized businesses" space following the purchase of a "20% equity stake" in YBL.

I have a favorable opinion of SMFG's decision to raise its interest in this Indian bank to 24% as revealed in Sep. It makes sense for the Japanese firm to gain greater "influence" over YBL and grow its financial contributions from this promising geographical market.

At its "Investor Relations" or "IR" Day in late-August, SMFG mentioned that "there are around 1,400 Japanese companies operating in India, about one‑tenth of the number in China."

Sumitomo can become the banker of choice for Japan's businesses that want to deepen their presence in India. Having a local associate like YBL would give it an edge over rivals in securing new business opportunities.

SMFG also took part in BofA's (BAC) "2025 Japan Conference" held in the prior month. Its slides prepared for this event cited third-party research suggesting that the "fee pool (for Indian commercial banks) is expected to grow 2.5x in 10 years."

YBL is a top-6 bank in India in terms of assets. It's well-placed to grab a sizeable part of the enlarged "fee pool" for the future as a leading domestic player.

The incremental 4% stake addition for "Yes Bank" implied a -5bps hit to SMFG's CET1. This wouldn't have been possible without the Kotak monetization, which strengthened its capital base.

Also, SA News reported on Sep 19 that "Jefferies and Sumitomo Mitsui" have engaged in "an expansion of their strategic alliance." This development sees "the Japan-based banking group raising its ownership in the former to up to 20% from 15%."

An Overview Of The SMFG-JEF Relationship

The main takeaway from SMFG purchasing additional JEF stock is the potential "Return On Equity" or "ROE" accretion. The Japanese bank's goal is to push up its group-level "Return On Equity" from 8% in the previous fiscal year (FY24) to 11% in the intermediate term.

SMFG's expectations are that the ¥0.135 trillion paid for 5% of Jefferies's shares will generate attractive ROEs of 13% and 17% in three and five years' time, respectively, from the time of investment.

Both Sumitomo and JEF are strong in their home markets. But it will be tougher for them to compete on their own with financial institutions with a global/international presence.

I believe that the two as a group will be able to win more mandates for cross-border deals going forward, as their relationship gets tighter. This provides support for SMFG's optimism regarding the returns of this increased stake.

Variant Views You Would Contemplate

Successful capital allocation is dependent on selling and buying at the right prices. If SMFG overpays for targets and sells its own assets on the cheap, that would be value-destructive.

Sumitomo's exposure to the U.S. and India has become more significant. If these two nations' economies deteriorate, this may affect its growth prospects.

My Conclusion

I continue to rate SMFG as a "Buy." The bank has been recycling capital in an active manner. It should eventually lead to an improvement in business portfolio quality and financial returns.

My fair P/B for Sumitomo is 1.33 times, implying a 27% (1.33/1.05) upside vis-a-vis its current multiple of 1.05x.

I have arrived at the figure by utilizing the "GGM (Gordon Growth Model) Valuation Formula." My ROE assumption is 11%, pegged to its above-mentioned mid-term target. The "Cost of Equity/k" and "Perpetuity Growth Rate/g" inputs are 9% (market average) and 3% (mature nation's long-term expansion rate), respectively.

data.title: EIS: Peace Could Pay Dividends For Israel And The Middle East
data.description:
Thesis

Could there finally be peace in the Middle East? The region seems to be turning corners in Syria, Lebanon, and Israel/Palestine. This could unleash economic growth across the region. Rebuilding in multiple countries, improving relations, and the potential for investments both domestic and abroad could provide tailwinds.

I covered iShares MSCI Israel ETF (NYSEARCA:EIS) in mid-August, rating it a buy. Since then, the ETF is up over 12%, well outpacing the gains of the SP500. I believe Israel’s economy and thus EIS could lock up further gains if the peace holds. Investments from the UAE and trade with Bahrain, which I explore below, both suggest what further regional cooperation may look like.

Israel has come under international pressure. For example, Norway’s sovereign wealth fund, a $2 trillion behemoth, divested from several Israeli companies. Peace could cool international pressure, which in turn could send EIS higher. Will the peace hold? Nothing is certain, but the prospects are the best they've been in recent memory, by my estimates. Reporting from the Wall Street Journal and other major news outlets paints a picture of an intensified and coordinated campaign to deliver lasting peace.

Israel Tech and Finance Could Enjoy Strong Growth

Israel has a wealthy, diverse, and well-educated population. Yet the population, while recording strong growth, is rather small at about 9.5 million. Such a small population would, on its own, struggle to support Israel's large and growing technology sector. Exports will likely prove crucial, especially in high-value areas like technology. Fortunately, many technology solutions and services are relatively easy to export. Shipping produce often requires refrigeration and other expenses. Exporting software and tech services may, in at least some cases, require little more than an Internet connection. Similarly, many financial services can be scaled across the globe relatively easily compared to physical goods. Yet exports, as shown below, have been on the decline. After a strong rebound before 2023, exports fell rather sharply.

With the global and regional geopolitical environment standing a good chance of improving, I believe that exports may resume, especially when it comes to technology. Israel is emerging as a finance and technology hub. Pinning down causation remains difficult, but it’s worth noting that in the first half of 2024, foreign investment bodies (i.e. venture capital funds) declined by 18% and while some of Israel’s big-cap tech companies have been doing well, many startups have struggled to secure funding and business.

As shown below, Israel's tech exports stalled in 2024. Until then, the sector had enjoyed solid growth. I suspect a durable peace would bolster growth. Tech workers and others have been called into service, thus reducing staff and disrupting operations. Israeli entrepreneurs have cited labor disruptions as making it difficult to complete projects. Peace should, in the long run, reduce disruptions.

Increased Regional Integration Could Spur Growth

Relations with Israel and many of its neighbors have been improving. Before October 7th, there were strong hopes that Israel and Saudi Arabia could start the process of normalization. With prospects of peace, I expect discussions to start again. As far as I know, direct investments from Saudi Arabia into Israel are not currently planned, but Saudi Arabia did invest $2 billion in Jared Kushner's fund, which in turn invests in Israel. Crucially, normalization with the UAE has brought economic cooperation, and could hint at what normalization with Saudi Arabia may look like.

Through the Abraham Accords, championed by the first Trump Administration, both the UAE and Bahrain normalized relations with Israel. The UAE also cemented a UAE-Israel Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement in 2023, and recently announced a $10 billion investment fund targeted at Israel. Israel opened an embassy in Bahrain and has pledged further economic cooperation. The Abraham Peace Accords Institute stated on Linkedin that trade increased between Israel and Bahrain by more than 1500% between July 2023 and July 2024 and notes that the accords could pave the way for more than $1 trillion in new economic activity and 4 million jobs across the region.

Further normalization, especially with large countries like Saudi Arabia, could pave the way for more investments, more trade, more jobs, and more travel. Israeli tech companies, in particular, may enjoy a strong infusion of investments, further supporting an already potent tech sector.

EIS Fundamentals and Valuation Considerations

Since I already covered EIS in an earlier article, taking a deeper dive into fundamentals and valuations, I’m going to focus on highlighting some key points. It’s worth revisiting my previous article if you want to take a closer look. I often take a close look at the largest equity holdings in an ETF, but with EIS, no holding makes up more than 9% and the portfolio has 106 equity holdings.

First, EIS has an attractive PE ratio right now at ~12, compared to a category average of ~15 (Morningstar). This PE ratio is made all the more attractive given that EIS holds substantial weight in technology stocks, which often carry higher PE numbers than other sectors. The iShares US Technology ETF (IYW), for example, has a PE ratio of nearly 30. The Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF), meanwhile, has a PE ratio north of 16. Price/sales is also solid at 1.93, although that’s a bit over the category average of 1.86 (Morningstar). Peace and integration could further drive sales and make it easier for Israeli companies to cultivate higher margins.

EIS’s dividend yield is only about 1%, and both the 3 and 5-year CAGR are in the red. If peace holds and integration occurs, I suspect that dividends will enjoy tailwinds as businesses secure further success. Still, for now, I wouldn’t count on EIS as an income ETF.

According to Morningstar, EIS has adjusted expensive fees of .59%, a touch high but in line with many country-specific ETFs. Saudi Arabia's iShares MSCI Saudi Arabia ETF (KSA) is substantially higher at .75%. The 3-year tracking error is also a bit high at 16.74% (Seeking Alpha). I admit, I'd like to see that go down, and the 1-year error rate fell to 15.99%. Still high, but improvement is always encouraging. Short interest is quite low at 3.87%. For comparison, KSA's short-interest comes in at 30.68%. (I am not a short seller, but I wouldn't bet against KSA. I did review the ETF and am a hold, for now, but I am keeping an eye on it.) All in all, Israel's risk profile strikes me as relatively low. Seeking Alpha's quant tools give it a C, but I'd probably give it a higher grade. Overall, the quant tools give a buy rating.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Quite simply, the peace may not hold. If so, this could stall investments and economic cooperation with neighbors. I would still likely rate EIS as a buy, but major tailwinds could be blown to the wind. I remain optimistic that conditions will improve, but geopolitical situations can be very unpredictable.

Key Takeaway: EIS Was a Buy Before, It’s More Of A Buy Now

I thought EIS was an attractive investment this past summer, and my outlook is improving. Especially if peace proves durable, the Israeli economy could be primed for a major boost as tech labor disruptions are reduced and economic relations with neighbors improve. Economic activity from the Abraham Accords is already promising and may be just a taste of what is to come. Israel’s tech and finance industries are very robust and innovative, and warming international relations could boost them further.

data.title: Bank of New York Mellon: Excellent Results Drive A Cycle-High Valuation
data.description:
Business appears to be going very well for The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation (NYSE:BK) these days. Being the largest of the three traditional custody banks, it naturally helps that capital markets have been very strong in recent months, while the bank's interest margin also remains near its recent historical high.

As a result, BK has been delivering some impressive operating leverage recently, which has been a boon for its bottom line. This has also rubbed off on its shares. Up around 20% since my opening piece back in June, BK has broadly matched the performance of its two closest peers/comps, comfortably beating the wider market.

Looking back, I did like BK's business profile for a spell of economic uncertainty, principally based on its limited credit risk compared to a "normal" commercial bank. However, concerns surrounding the stock's valuation kept me neutral, which was evidently a poor call. BK's recently released Q3 results provide a nice opportunity to revisit that position. While there is a lot to like about the firm's recent financial performance, the stock's valuation remains at a cycle-high level. Accordingly, I am leaving my Hold rating in place.

Firing On All Cylinders

For the third quarter, BK posted adjusted earnings per share of $1.91, handily beating consensus. At just around $5.1 billion, adjusted revenue also came in higher than expected. While adjusted EPS fell $0.03 sequentially, it was still up an exceptional 26% year-on-year.

Buoyant capital markets have certainly helped to a degree. Per above, BK is the largest custodian. It held $41.7 trillion in assets under custody/administration in its Securities Services segment at the end of Q3. As such, a portion of its fee revenue will be linked to asset levels. Because there is a lag in billing, it probably makes more sense to look at the changes in the prior-quarter ending levels rather than the most recent quarter. On that basis, AUC/A was up around 12% year-on-year and 5% quarter-on-quarter.

Historically, this line has been affected by fee margin compression. The short story is that BK's clients have been facing their own cost headwinds, forcing them to look for savings in areas like custody expenses.

One reason I think shares of BK and its peers have done so well recently is that there are signs that this pressure is abating. Indeed, management said as much earlier in the year. There is evidence of this in the bank's recent results. In Q3, asset servicing fees in this part of the business came in at $1.141 billion, up 4% quarter-on-quarter and 12% year-on-year. Per above, that is pretty close to its AUC/A growth.

Meanwhile, BK is also seeing a strong cycle-related boost to its net interest income ("NII"). This line came in at $1.236 billion last quarter, up 18% year-on-year and 3% quarter-on-quarter. Driving this, the bank continues to see a modest tailwind from asset repricing, with maturing securities being reinvested at higher prevailing yields. Developments on the liability side look positive as well. Specifically, BK has recently grown non-interest-bearing deposits, the most attractive source of funding, faster than its overall deposits. NIB balances averaged $51.13 billion last quarter, or 13.8% of its total funding mix. This was up around 40 basis points quarter-on-quarter and 15 basis points year-on-year. At the same time, yields on interest-bearing liabilities have been falling due to lower interest rates.

The upshot of these trends is that BK's net interest margin remains near its recent historical high point. It clocked in at 1.31% last quarter, up 4 basis points sequentially and 15 basis points year-on-year.

Now, one of the features of this business is that certain types of revenue growth can drive a lot of operating leverage. NIM-driven growth is a great example. If BK's NIM expands, it doesn't follow that the company needs to go out and spend more on labor or office space. That revenue growth largely flows down to its pre-tax profit.

This trend has been a boon for BK's bottom line recently. Indeed, while revenue rose over 9% year-on-year in Q3, operating costs increased by a more modest 4%. On the back of this, pre-provision income rose ~20% year-on-year to $1.85 billion.

Unsurprisingly, the bank's underlying profitability has followed suit, with BK reporting an exceptional 25.6% return on tangible common equity for Q3. As you can see, that represents one of its strongest quarterly prints in recent years.

Other Things To Like

Last time, I mentioned a couple of other aspects of BK's business that I liked, and I wanted to touch on these in light of recent results. The first is the bank's ongoing capital returns to investors. Given the nature of its business and its high ROTCE, BK doesn't need to retain a large share of its earnings. This leaves it free to spend heavily on dividends and buybacks instead. Indeed, BK did around $1.2 billion in capital returns last quarter, equal to a ~90% payout ratio.

This takes year-to-date distributions to around $3.5 billion, and this has skewed roughly 70% towards buybacks. Of course, this is boosting various per-share metrics, with shares outstanding declining around 4% year-on-year. Per above, adjusted EPS rose by over 25% year-on-year last quarter, while tangible book value per share increased by 9% to $30.60.

The second point I wanted to circle back on was credit risk, or rather, the lack thereof. While stock markets have been strong, it's not like economic uncertainty has disappeared. In that sense, BK may be a nice name to own relative to other banks. Whereas a typical commercial bank will hold around 65% of interest-earning assets in loans, BK's circa $75 billion loan book only accounted for around 20% of its earning assets last quarter. In contrast, it holds a relatively high share of earning assets in cash and investment securities, 99% of which are rated 'AA-' or above. Credit quality also remains excellent. BK recorded just $4 million in net charge-offs last quarter, while it had just $160 million in non-performing assets, down around 25% from the year-ago quarter.

The Valuation

Like last time, the only real issue I have with BK is its valuation. The stock trades for just under $109 as I type, equal to around 3.55x its tangible book value per share. Based on period-end figures, that is the richest the stock has been in the past 30 quarters. Its average over this period was closer to 2.2x TBVPS.

So, while BK is enjoying a cycle-high ROTCE right now, that is being met with a commensurately rich multiple from the market, leaving investors exposed to the risk of possible mean reversion. Given the lack of a margin of safety, I remain cautious, and I am leaving my Hold rating in place.

data.title: McDonald's Has A Strategic Business Model And Dividends, But Risks And Overvaluation Exist
data.description:
Whenever I think of a fast-food or quick-service restaurant, McDonald's Corporation (NYSE:MCD) often comes first to my mind. Of course, that is not always the case for everyone else since we have different preferences and tastes. In my defense, I think this is the most popular brand. And maybe it's impossible that you've never heard of it before unless you're living in a very secluded place.

Over the years, McDonald's Corporation has cemented its place in the global restaurant industry. With over 40,000 stores under its name, it maintains a huge domestic and international presence, which makes it a highly competitive player. Its continued expansion remains fruitful amid sustained revenue growth and margin expansion.

However, I have some concerns about its potential overexpansion that may lead to same-store cannibalization. On top of that, external factors are still not favorable at this point. This is why I think MCD has already had a good run, and further stock price increase does not seem justifiable anymore. Technicals are still bullish, but early warnings are emerging.

MCD's Strong Points

An easy answer would be its strong brand recognition. McDonald's Corporation is nearly ubiquitous since it is present on all continents. Considering this, it's not surprising that its revenue remains strong. In Q2 2025, its operating revenue reached $6.84B, up by 5.4% YoY from $6.49B. At first glance, you may think that it was only due to its continued store openings. After all, its total systemwide restaurants increased from 42,406 to 44,113 units as of this writing. But if you look at it carefully, MCD remains in demand and productive. In fact, its average sales in company-owned restaurants reached $1.20M per unit ($4.2M sales/2,054 units) versus $1.15M YoY.

Another thing I like about MCD is its strategic business model due to its high concentration on franchised restaurants. Franchising is not as costly and capital-intensive as company-operated restaurants. They are also less cost-sensitive than adding owned restaurants. So even if inflation remains stubborn, MCD can control cost pressures. As a result, it still enjoys a double-digit operating margin at 47.3% versus 45.0% YoY. Its franchising segment is still well-positioned as MCD also enjoys a higher franchise fee per unit at $100,169 versus $98,918 YoY.

Considering all this, it's not surprising that its operations remain sustainable. As we can see, its operating cash flow remains enough to cover its capex.

Challenges

Despite its strong market positioning, MCD faces several challenges that may hamper its growth.

1. Inflation Remains Stubborn. As a quick-service restaurant, inflation is one of its biggest challenges because QSRs like MCD capitalize on cheaper pricing. As cost pressures increase, it will be hard for prices and sales to offset them. This can squeeze margins if not handled properly. Currently, it's at 2.9%, a notable increase from its stable trend at 2.3%-2.4% before the uptick.

Aside from that, QSRs are unintentionally facing some levels of competition with fast-casual diners because their price differentiation has narrowed due to higher prices on the menu. We know that fast-casual diners capitalize on value propositions like premium ingredients. With that, competition may become more predatory.

2. Unfavorable Consumer Perception May Lower Its Competitive Edge. Despite the popularity and huge global presence, customers do not seem fond of McDonald's. In a recent study, MCD's customer satisfaction index for 2025 is only 70, down from 71 in 2024. It is also at the bottom of burger chains. Its close peers like Burger King of Restaurant Brands International Inc. (QSR) enjoy a relatively high score of 77. Even if we include other chains like coffee and pizza, MCD will still be at the bottom.

If we look at this observation in geographical diversification, Culver's and Burger King lead the group. While MCD capitalizes on brand recognition and expansion, its competitors enjoy high customer satisfaction. This may affect MCD's market positioning and foot traffic in the long run.

3. Overexpanding May Pit Its Stores Against One Another And Affect Sustainability. McDonald's continued store expansion and increased franchising concentration continue to work to its favor. Its revenue and margins were solid last Q2 2025. However, this may not always be the case as inflation still signals economic uncertainty. On top of that, the plan to open hundreds of stores by 2027 may pose a threat to its growth in the long run. While it can increase brand recognition and accessibility to foot traffic, new stores may cannibalize the demand and sales of existing ones. This may hurt not only the company-operated units but also its franchisees. To make it simple, a portion of existing stores' sales will just go to new stores.

The thing is, MCD should ensure the total addressable market. By doing so, it will avoid overexpansion. I'm not saying that it's not doing it since I have no evidence to prove it. But what it's doing right now may be risky. While I understand that it also anticipates economic stability and improved spending in the next few years, MCD may be bleeding out too much cash or borrowings.

If we look at its Balance Sheet, MCD already has over $40B in debt, a substantial increase in the past 11 quarters. The consolation is that 99% of the total amount has a longer maturity. This is supported by its Net Debt/EBITDA of 2.8x, which means that MCD only needs about three years to repay the whole amount at once even if it doesn't have to.

The challenge is that it remains heavily reliant on debt issuance if we look at its cash flow statement. Without this, its FCF cannot cover debt repayments after paying dividends and stock repurchases. Otherwise, it will deplete its cash level.

4. Its Price Cuts Can Broaden Price War And May Squeeze Margins. Another strategy of McDonald's to drive foot traffic is price cuts on the menu. This can help lower the cannibalization risk I explained earlier. It may be confident enough considering its sales growth and margins as it focuses more on franchising than running owned stores. However, inflationary headwinds are still intense. In addition, this may hurt the margins of franchisees. Of course, MCD is not immediately affected since it gets its cut directly from sales. Yet, it must reassess the situation of its domestic franchisees to ensure their capacity to sustain these price discounts.

Valuation

To assess MCD more, I used different valuation methods. The first one is the price ratio using EPS and sales per share. The stock price has been quite reactive with these two financial metrics in some years. For instance, the average stock price increased from $200.83 in 2020 to $234.48 in 2021 and to $253.78 in 2022 when the EPS increased from $2.66 to $2.80 and to $3.13, respectively. The same applies to sales per share. However, the stock price still increased in 2023 and 2024 even when these metrics weakened in those years. Now, it has already reached the $300 mark, which can be justified by the notable increase in the EPS and sales per share.

If we look at its P/E, MCD trades at a very high multiple of 95.58x versus the five-year average of 89.71x. From there, we can say that the market does not seem to care whether MCD trades at a very high valuation or multiple. As long as its financial metrics improve, the price will follow. But again, it's already very expensive. If we use the average value to get its fair value, the target price or TP will be $280.80. The same applies to P/S, which suggests a TP of $270.96. If we add a 5% risk consideration, the TP will decrease.

To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. Dividends are another important aspect of MCD. That is why I don't necessarily recommend a sell rating despite its severe overpricing. Its dividends of $7.08 with a payout ratio of just 58.56% show that it earns enough to sustain itself. To do this model, I also included the dividend growth rate of 7.20%. Meanwhile, I estimated its cost of equity using the capital asset pricing model. As you can see, I set its risk premium at 7.0%, which is much higher than the typical 5% because of the risks I explained earlier. Lastly, I estimated Beta on MS Excel using the SLOPE function of the daily price change versus the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, the TP will be $284.14. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $269.87.

Lastly, I used its Enterprise Value or EV of $268.17B versus revenue estimates for 2025. I expect its YoY rebound in Q1 and Q2 2025 to continue in Q3 and Q4. So, I am setting my revenue growth expectations from 2% to 5%. This will also serve as my reference for my high and low estimates. Using 5%, I derived a revenue of $27.2B. For 2%, the revenue will be $26.4B. My EV/Revenue high and low will be 9.87x and 10.16x. Their midpoint is 10.01x. Meanwhile, the reported EV/Revenue TTM and FWD are 10.29x and 10.03x with a midpoint of 10.16x. If we look at its FWD, MCD is trading quite above the reasonable level. Clearly, it has already priced in my growth expectations. However, it's already a bit too much. If we use the midpoints to derive my TP, MCD should be trading at a maximum price of $295.53. If I add a 5% risk consideration, it will be $280.76.

Technicals

MCD's technicals are quite flattish today. The stock price demonstrates rebound attempts at $300 after its recent dip to $293. Now, it is already below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, which suggests that the uptrend may not hold any longer. We should note that the most recent high at $317 did not break above the previous high of $320, so momentum may not be strong enough for another rally. The 50-day SMA is flattening again near the 200-day SMA, which means that momentum has neutralized recently.

Its MACD and RSI reflect the price trend. The MACD line is still within negative territory, which means that selling strength still outpaces buyers. But its uptrend after crossing above the signal line and the histogram within positive territory shows the buying splurge in the past four days. This was mainly due to the recent overselling, as shown by RSI. Now, it's at 54, which is already within the normal index range. This can explain the neutral trend of the 50-day SMA. Considering this, there is quite limited room for buying. So, I suggest waiting for another dip or preferably overselling before taking a position. Once the index approaches 70, dips may take place again. With that, investors may have to stay on the sidelines.

Conclusion

McDonald's Corporation stays resilient with its strong brand recognition and strategic business model. However, risks related to intensifying competition, overexpansion, same-store cannibalization tendencies, and stubborn inflation must be considered. Valuation adheres to it as the stock appears overpriced at this point. Technicals are normal, but buying opportunities are limited since the price strength index has bounced back from the recent overselling. I'm recommending a hold rating on the stock for newcomers. For existing investors, they may choose to lighten their holdings to minimize risks and take profit. But selling all may not be a prudent choice due to its stable and sustainable dividends.

data.title: Nestlé: Upside From Improved Costs But Valuation Dictates Hold (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Nestle (OTCPK:NSRGY) reported 9M sales today, and there are a number of items in this earnings report to focus on. The market confirms this, with a significant share price movement today for the Switzerland-native ticker.

The company, like many FMCG businesses, has seen pressure from increased costs coupled with increased inflation and customers having "less money". This has forced many of the companies, not only in Nestle's position but also in grocer positions like Axfood (OTCPK:AXFOY) to be forced to adapt. Overall, I believe the vast majority of grocers on the market in the EU today are overvalued. Many of my positions are divested at this time, with some exceptions for cheap businesses at a high quality, including French Carrefour (OTCPK:CRARY).

If we look at Nestlé's historical valuation evolution, it's very clear that the company's current share price, together with other trends, marks a clear move towards undervaluation in the context of a premium. The question becomes, how much do we discount the company for its current forecasted lack of excessive or impressive EPS growth?

Nestle, which typically grows at least 3-4% per year, has spent the last fiscal dropping 1%. What's more, as of the 9M period, the analyst's current expectations for the company include a close-to-double-digit drop in adjusted EPS towards a 4.34CHF level. This would be lower than earnings have been in almost 5 years. Not until 2026-2027 are earnings expected to improve once again.

Nestle is currently AA-rated, yielding just south of 4%, varying today depending on your entry. My cost basis has me at about flat at today's price, and my YoC is 3.9%.

In this article, I will update my thesis based on the 3Q25/9M results.

Nestlé - Upside remains if you can endure 2025, and wait for upside

The new CEO "came out swinging", I would argue. He's taken an argument of forward transparency. While the nine-month sales growth of 3.3% was good, especially given that it's organic and 3Q results saw the previously negative trends turn positive, there is a lot left to do. In particular, what worries me is the excessive premiumization that Nestlé has enjoyed over time.

It's fairly clear to me that, lacking growth and with inflation and costs going up, Nestlé should probably not be considered at such high premiums any longer.

My last article on Nestle showed me considering "Nestle being on sale". You can find it here, and it's roughly a year ago. However, it hasn't outperformed the SPY, despite a good price and entry for me.

So what has been going on? One of the issues I've had with valuing Nestlé is that it's been very hard to see exactly what the company has planned in terms of savings, targets, and expectations. Things have been opaque - both concrete goals, but above all, how to achieve them.

As of this very morning, we have more clarity.

The company plans to reduce 16,000 people. That's a big chunk, but it does make up less than 20% of the company's total workforce, so that's worth considering. Of this, over 10,000 positions are white-collar employees, meaning reducing SG&A, which I'm sure has to do with digitization as well. This, in turn, is expected to save about 1B CHF in costs, which has the net result of bringing about a higher savings target by as much as 500M CHF. The original plan investors were presented with did not specify the number of headcount reductions. It does now.

The second positive, the company plans to continue its internal growth strategy, which has already improved in terms of momentum and proven to bring about company results.

It's important, in my view, not to overestimate the impact or effect of these quarterly results. This savings target is a good one, but I remind you that this company has been in a consistently negative trend over the medium term for going on 3 years now. Turning around this "tanker in this puddle" will take some time.

The main challenge Nestlé has to overcome is bringing real growth. That's easy when you're a fresh startup, not so much when you're one of the biggest food companies on the planet. Price increases have made the company's products less popular, and the company has reported operational issues, which I have described in previous articles. Furthermore, I will go ahead and firmly argue that the entire food sector is becoming more and more commoditized. In different countries, such as Sweden, private store labels are taking over more and more, and where store labels aren't doing so, smaller brands, which are more local, with less footprint, but far less of a cost base and more agile, are competing with Nestlé.

So, plenty of challenges.

However, Nestlés positives remain a compelling investment argument. The diversity and geographic reach of the products this company offers are second to none. Competition with products and brands like Nespresso is difficult; it has incredible moats. A huge portion, over 55% of Nestlés operating profit, comes from areas where it's evident from sales that the company has not only pricing power, but consumer confidence, and the penetration in this area in terms of private label is close to zilch. No one has come with a "store label espresso pod" - not yet, at the very least. And the company being entrenched in the way that it is, there isn't much that can compete with that.

So, where is Nestlé facing competition exactly - what products can we expect the company to be looking more at?

Pure dairy and VAP-meals (prepared dishes) for one. Volume growth is very clearly not recovering here, but despite this, the company is maintaining its 2025E guidance, which, by the way, I view as being far too optimistic at the time being.

I expect that it will take some time to really bring about better volume growth and reinvigorate the company here. This will be especially hard in underperforming business areas, which Nestlé clearly has (as you can see above). That's why the company is increasing marketing spend to about 9% of sales by the end of this fiscal, in part paid for by the leaving employees. To give you an idea, this was 7.5% of sales in 2024.

Whenever you have a company that generates over $90B in annual sales of products, it takes a lot of money to move that needle - but when a company shifts 150 bps somewhere, that amounts to hundreds and hundreds of millions.

In the end, despite the risks that I view as justified in receiving more investor focus at this time, Nestlé remains the world's largest packaged food company - and this sort of operation neither disappears nor deserves being devalued on the count of some value and pricing pressure. I go by fundamentals. And fundamentals tell us that ROIC has averaged over 16%, more than 100% higher than the estimated sub-7% WACC. The lower returns we're seeing tell us that the premium should drop a bit, but not that this company is worth less than before.

For that reason, I believe that my thesis from 2024 still holds, with some adjustments.

Valuation for Nestlé - Upside is slimmer, but still there if you premiumize the company.

I gave the company a 20x P/E average over time in my last article, which at this time comes to about 100 CHF for 2027E. In this article, I will use the latest volume-pressured results to lower this target by 10%, and down to 90 CHF in taking some headroom for further challenges we're seeing in several markets. This means we're moving away from the 20-year P/E average, and moving to discounting the company's averages, which is the first time I've done this for Nestlé.

On the basis of a 90-95 CHF upside, the company at today's valuation of 82 CHF (at the time of writing this article), no longer has a 15% or above annualized rate of return. However, because I believe the company's fair value is easily, in the long term, 110-115 CHF once they get these things under control, the rating change does not mean that I am selling my stake. It merely means that I am not buying more. I'm allowing for Nestle to "ripen" as an investment at this time, meaning I'm letting the share price appreciate over time until I feel it's time to exit.

So does this mean it's too late to buy the company?

That depends. Are you willing to give the company a premium for its market position and plans for savings and expansion? If so, you may put Nestlé at a higher PT and target than me, and in doing so, justify a higher PT of 100 CHF. Other analysts are doing so, after all. Look at these ranges for PTs from others.

But you will notice that there's not a whole lot of analysts going higher than 100 CHF.

Therefore, I argue that my PT is actually in line with what can be expected here.

I view Nestle, therefore, as a "HOLD", not a "BUY" any longer, and change my rating as of this article.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risks for Nestlé remain competition in its market segments where the company does not have a moat, which amounts to 40-45% of its overall income base. In continued and rising competition, the potential for further downside here is not trivial. Many of Nestlés products are further related to either coffee or chocolate, and input cost increases for cacao beans and coffee beans have been high for the past few years. This is likely to weigh on operating margins, as I believe Nestlé is unable to pass on these costs in their entirety onto consumers.

And furthermore, no matter how you "slice" Nestlé, the company is the largest business of its kind in the world. Even if it were to launch a new blockbuster-type 1B CHF product, that would move the sales by less than 2%, and it's hard to come up with blockbuster-type products. What I want to focus on here is that the bigger a company gets, the harder it is for that company to really grow bigger.

Based on this, I change my PT for the company to 90 CHF in terms of PT, and my rating to a "HOLD". This means that for ADR NSRGY, the PT is $114/share.

Thesis

The company is one of the most fundamentally appealing food companies on the face of the earth. The company has one of the most impressive food operations on the planet, and for that reason, I consider it to be a quality business at the right price, though recently, the price for this company has increased a bit too much.

Low debt, a decent dividend, and a decent upside at the right price make this a "BUY" at below 19-20X average P/E.

As of this article, I am lowering my PT to 90 CHF for the native ticker, and also lowering my rating for Nestlé to "HOLD". The company is better waited on here, either to rise or to drop.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (bolded).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
